questions.q: In which field of mathematics did Srinivasa Ramanujan make his most famous contributions?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan is celebrated above all for his deep results in number theory, including partitions and infinite series.

questions.q: Srinivasa Ramanujan was a citizen of which country?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan was an Indian mathematician, born in the Madras Presidency of British India.

questions.q: Which famous English mathematician became Ramanujan's mentor and collaborator at Cambridge?
questions.explanation: G. H. Hardy invited Ramanujan to Cambridge after recognising his genius from a letter Ramanujan sent in 1913.

questions.q: Ramanujan is often described as a mathematician who was largely:
questions.explanation: Ramanujan was essentially self-taught, developing much of his mathematics with almost no formal higher training.

questions.q: At which English university did Ramanujan work alongside G. H. Hardy?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan came to Trinity College, Cambridge, in 1914 to work with Hardy.

questions.q: Ramanujan's life is famously short, ending when he died at around what age?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan died in 1920 at the age of 32, cutting short an extraordinarily productive career.

questions.q: A famous anecdote involves a taxicab numbered 1729. What is special about 1729?
options: It is the smallest number expressible as a sum of two cubes in two different ways
It cannot be written as any sum of cubes
questions.explanation: Ramanujan noted 1729 is the smallest number expressible as the sum of two positive cubes in two distinct ways (1+1728 and 729+1000).

questions.q: The 2015 biographical film about Ramanujan shares its title with a famous book about him. What is that title?
questions.explanation: Robert Kanigel's biography and the 2015 film are both titled 'The Man Who Knew Infinity.'

questions.q: Ramanujan was elected a Fellow of which prestigious British scientific society in 1918?
questions.explanation: In 1918 Ramanujan became one of the youngest Fellows of the Royal Society, honoured for his work in number theory.

questions.q: Much of Ramanujan's early original mathematics was recorded by him in personal documents known as his:
questions.explanation: Ramanujan filled several notebooks with thousands of results, which mathematicians have studied for decades.

questions.q: In which year did Ramanujan write the famous first letter to G. H. Hardy that launched their collaboration?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan's letter, dated 16 January 1913, contained startling results that convinced Hardy of his genius.

questions.q: In which city was Ramanujan born in 1887?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan was born on 22 December 1887 in Erode, in present-day Tamil Nadu.

questions.q: Ramanujan and Hardy are jointly credited with a famous asymptotic formula for which quantity?
options: The number of partitions of an integer
questions.explanation: The Hardy–Ramanujan asymptotic formula gives the approximate number of partitions of a large integer n.

questions.q: Before going to Cambridge, Ramanujan worked as a clerk at which Indian institution?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan held a clerk's post at the Madras Port Trust, where supporters recognised and encouraged his talent.

questions.q: India's National Mathematics Day is observed on 22 December in honour of Ramanujan because that date is his:
questions.explanation: National Mathematics Day in India marks Ramanujan's birthday, 22 December 1887.

questions.q: Which special functions, central to modern work on modular forms, did Ramanujan describe in his final 1920 letter to Hardy?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan introduced the 'mock theta functions' in his last letter to Hardy, anticipating a field developed much later.

questions.q: Hardy famously devised an informal scale of mathematical talent. On a scale of 100, how did he reportedly rate Ramanujan?
questions.explanation: Hardy rated himself 25 and Ramanujan 100, calling their collaboration the one romantic incident of his life.

questions.q: Numbers like 1729 that are the smallest expressible as a sum of two cubes in n distinct ways are now sometimes called:
questions.explanation: Inspired by the 1729 anecdote, such numbers are called 'taxicab numbers' in number theory.

questions.q: Ramanujan is well known for producing remarkable rapidly converging infinite series for which mathematical constant?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan found infinite series for 1/π that converge extremely fast and underlie modern algorithms for computing π.

questions.q: In which year did Ramanujan first arrive in England to begin his work at Cambridge?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan sailed to England and arrived in 1914, beginning roughly five intense years of collaboration with Hardy.

questions.q: Ramanujan was elected a Fellow of Trinity College in 1918, making him one of the first:
options: Indians elected to a Trinity College fellowship
questions.explanation: Ramanujan was among the first Indians to be elected a Fellow of Trinity College, Cambridge.

questions.q: Which English mathematician, besides Hardy, frequently collaborated with Ramanujan at Cambridge?
questions.explanation: J. E. Littlewood worked closely with Hardy and helped assess and develop Ramanujan's results.

questions.q: A 'lost notebook' of Ramanujan's later results was famously rediscovered in 1976 by which mathematician?
questions.explanation: George Andrews discovered the 'lost notebook' among papers in the Wren Library at Trinity College in 1976.

questions.q: The Hardy–Ramanujan theorem concerns the number of distinct prime factors of a typical integer n. What is its 'normal order'?
questions.explanation: The Hardy–Ramanujan theorem shows that a typical integer n has about log log n distinct prime factors.

questions.q: Which arithmetic function, important in the theory of modular forms, did Ramanujan introduce and study, leading to a famous conjecture later proved by Pierre Deligne?
questions.explanation: Ramanujan's tau function arises from the modular discriminant, and the Ramanujan conjecture about it was proved by Deligne in 1974.

questions.q: Ramanujan discovered striking congruences for the partition function p(n), including that p(5n+4) is always divisible by:
questions.explanation: One of Ramanujan's celebrated partition congruences states that p(5n + 4) is divisible by 5 for all n.

questions.q: Hardy and Ramanujan's partition asymptotics relied on a powerful analytic technique later refined by Hans Rademacher into an exact formula. This method is known as the:
questions.explanation: The Hardy–Ramanujan (and Hardy–Littlewood) circle method underlies the asymptotic and later exact formulas for partitions.

questions.q: Ramanujan's work on highly composite numbers studied integers that have more divisors than any:
questions.explanation: A highly composite number, studied in Ramanujan's 1915 paper, has more divisors than any smaller positive integer.

questions.q: Several elegant identities for sums of certain hypergeometric-type series, such as Ramanujan's identities for continued fractions, are linked to which named continued fraction?
questions.explanation: The Rogers–Ramanujan continued fraction and identities are among Ramanujan's deepest contributions to q-series.

questions.q: Ramanujan's illness in England was long attributed to tuberculosis, but later medical analysis suggested a more likely diagnosis of:
questions.explanation: Later reassessment by physician D. A. B. Young suggested Ramanujan more likely suffered from hepatic amoebiasis, a treatable amoebic liver infection.
